comments.text: I've managed to get with GPT-5.4 Pro the following proof of the first statement, basically showing that the classical Specker graph already gives the positive answer.

I ran the standard check which found 1 minor issue. The result is a bit stronger than $n^{1-\varepsilon}$; we have $\alpha(H) \gg n/\log n$ which seems important to note. GPT-5.4 Thinking remarked that this result felt like "an overlooked corollary".

Thanks; this problem continues to be strange - it already came up in the Aletheia case study, in which their AI refound essentially the construction from [EHS82], but then it was noted that the assumption that $G$ have $\aleph_1$ vertices was missing. In this case, as you say exactly this construciton was already studied in [EHS82], and they seem to have calculated the upper bound but not the lower bound, depsite this being a reasonably standard calculation. I suspect that they probably already knew at the time that this behaved like $n^{1-o(1)}$ - I note that in both [EHS82] and [Er95d] just the stronger version of $\gg n$ is asked, with no mention of the $\gg n^{1-\epsilon}$ version. I think that maybe Erdős had just forgotten that they already knew about the $\gg n^{1-\epsilon}$ version when he asked this in [Er95]. It would be good to find somewhere in the literature, which I suspect exists, that does this lower bound calculation already.
